ops.title: wire server: v3 persistent sessions, dispatch classes, trust floor

ops.body:
# v3 session architecture (v2 shipped in 7623022; bb42d99 bumped ProtocolVersion to 3 — "bounded multiplexed" — and added wire/stream.go; the v1 LF-framed dispatch at bottom is historical)

Server serves persistent multiplexed exact-v3 sessions (length-prefixed frames, wire/frame.go) on a caller-owned listener it never binds. Note: the lifeproto ENVELOPE version is independent of the transport and remains {"v":2}. accept first reserves a MaxSessions slot — a full server closes the connection immediately, before any read. A slotted connection then runs: UnixConn assertion → PeerFromConn (identity captured once) → trust check → mandatory Hello/HelloAck handshake bounded by HandshakeTimeout. Hello strictness: FrameHello with ID 0, Sequence 0, Flags == FlagEnd, empty Op and Tenant, DeadlineUnixMilli 0, non-empty payload; decodeStrict rejects unknown fields and trailing JSON; exact ProtocolVersion; non-empty Build. Each session runs one readLoop plus one writer goroutine draining a bounded outbound channel (OutboundQueue); Codec reads are single-goroutine, writes serialized and safe from any goroutine.

Dispatch: every accepted request runs in its OWN goroutine (receiveRequest launches go execute). The two classes differ only in pooling: **control handlers skip the worker pool — multiple control handlers can overlap**, they are NOT serialized on the read loop; concurrent handlers acquire the shared bounded pool (Workers slots + Backlog queue), and past capacity the request is Rejected — a proven non-dispatch, safe to replay. (v1's exclusive class and tenant-gate serialization did not survive; tenant rides the envelope only.) Duplicate request IDs are refused per session (seen set + watermark → ErrDuplicateID); stream chunks are sequence-checked (ErrStreamOrder); InboundQueue caps active request IDs per session and StreamQueue caps unread inbound chunks per request. A request context is bounded by the EARLIER of the client-supplied Frame.DeadlineUnixMilli and the Ladder server deadline, and cancels on a cancel frame, disconnect, or server shutdown (and when execute finishes).

**Lifecycle ops do NOT bypass admission.** RegisterLifecycle handlers skip only build-mismatch and drain rejection — so a shutdown request is still acknowledged mid-drain — but they participate in ordinary admission (pinned by TestLifecycleSkipsDrainRejectionButParticipatesInAdmission). The lifecycle.go godoc originally overstated this as "bypass ordinary admission"; the sweep reworded it.

**Admit hook**: Serve's admit runs for every request that clears the pre-admission checks (unknown op, inbound-queue/ID-window overflow, build mismatch, draining) — so it also runs for requests admit itself rejects and for concurrent requests later Rejected by a full Workers+Backlog pool. After successful admission, done runs when execute returns: a successful terminal send waits for writer acknowledgement first, but streaming cancellation can abort before any terminal send is attempted, and done still runs.

# Trust floor (invariant unchanged from v1)

The same-effective-UID floor runs on every accepted connection, and a non-nil Trust callback augments the floor, never replaces it (ErrUntrustedPeer). Timing moved: v1 checked trust after the first frame read so a denial could deliver a rejection; v2+ checks BEFORE the handshake reads any frame — a rejected peer gets no session and no reply.

# Shutdown

CloseIntake stops new connections and new ordinary requests; accepted sessions stay alive so admitted work and lifecycle acknowledgements can settle. v1's watchDisconnect short-read-deadline poke is gone: the persistent readLoop observes EOF/reset directly (isDisconnect) and tears down the session's active requests.

# Historical (v1, replaced in 7623022)

LF-framed JSON, one request per connection; three classes (control/concurrent/exclusive) plus per-tenant capacity-1 gates taken on the connection goroutine; watchDisconnect poked the read side under a short deadline while the handler ran; requestGrace/writeGrace bounded the pre-frame read and the response write. Retained here only as design rationale for why the session protocol folded these into the read loop.
